policy_statement: Agriculture adaptation strategy: strengthen Ministry of Agriculture capacity; improve integrated pest/disease/weed management and resistant varieties; assess crop vulnerability/suitability by agro-ecological zones; and enhance climate-proof agro-infrastructure to raise farmer productivity.
mechanism_description: The strategy strengthens agricultural institutions, improves pest/disease/weed management and crop suitability assessment, and enhances climate-proof farm infrastructure to reduce climate-related production risks and improve farmer productivity.
primary_causal_pathway: Climate hazard variability/extremes → improved farm/institutional capacity + resilient practices/infrastructure → ↓ exposure/sensitivity of agriculture → ↓ climate risk impacts (yield loss, livelihood disruption)
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Changes the agricultural system by strengthening extension/governance capacity, deploying integrated pest/disease/weed management and resistant varieties, using agro-ecological suitability assessments for crop choices, and investing in climate-resilient farm and agro-infrastructure (e.g., drainage, irrigation, storage, roads). (2) These measures buffer farms against climate stressors and climate-amplified biotic pressures by improving preparedness, suitability of cropping systems to local conditions, and robustness of physical systems that manage water, access, and post-harvest losses. (3) The climate-relevant outcome is reduced climate-driven production volatility and losses (higher and more stable yields/incomes under climate change), i.e., improved adaptive capacity and resilience.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced vulnerability of agricultural production and livelihoods to climate hazards through improved resilience practices and climate-proof infrastructure.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: climate hazards (and climate-amplified pests/diseases) threaten yields → program improves resilience practices, institutions, and infrastructure → reduces sensitivity/exposure and stabilizes production.  
2) Why Adaptation over alternatives: the central intent and effect is risk reduction and resilience (reduced damages from climate impacts), not a direct, primary reduction in GHG emissions (Mitigation), not mainly “same service with less resource input” (Resource Efficiency), and not primarily ecosystem restoration/carbon sinks (Nature-Based Solutions).  
3) Edge cases applied: some implementations may include mitigation co-benefits (reduced deforestation pressure, reduced losses) or efficiency gains (water/inputs), but these are contingent and not the single biggest effect across all policies using this mechanism.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: any “climate-resilient agriculture program” focused on climate-proofing practices/infrastructure and managing climate-linked pests/diseases has the dominant effect of lowering climate risk to agriculture, consistent with Adaptation.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment (e.g., drought resilience, flood management, pest management, livelihood resilience) is deferred to policy-level evidence and is not determined at the mechanism-only classification step.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If a specific program is primarily justified/implemented as emissions mitigation (e.g., fertilizer N2O reduction, methane reduction in rice/livestock) or as ecosystem restoration (e.g., agroforestry explicitly for carbon sequestration), it may warrant a different primary category at the policy level; however, the stated mechanism here is predominantly climate risk reduction and productivity stabilization (adaptation).
key_indicators: “Adaptation strategies”; “Strengthen the capacity of the Ministry of Agriculture”; “training of experts, logistics, and use of technology”; “integrated pest and pathogen management”; “Assess crops vulnerability and suitability… for different Agroecological zones”; “Enhance climate-proof agro-infrastructural systems”
co_benefits: Food security and rural income gains via “increase productivity”; general institutional modernization/technology uptake beyond climate adaptation.
instance_edge_case_notes: Includes an infrastructure component (“Enhance climate-proof agro-infrastructural systems”) that could be coded as infrastructure-investment if treated as a funded build/upgrade program; however, the excerpt is framed as a list of strategies without specific investment commitments, budgets, or project directives, so planning-strategy is retained.
